subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Super-Earths
description: A quick, practical guide to Super-Earths.

hook.text: Super-Earths are planets between 1 and 10 Earth masses. Our solar system has none, but they're the most common type found.

buildup.text: They might be rocky like Earth or wrapped in thick hydrogen envelopes. We don't know for sure yet.

discovery.text: Kepler found that super-Earths are extremely common around other stars. Their absence here is the real mystery.

twist.text: Jupiter may have prevented super-Earths from forming in our solar system by disrupting their building materials early on.

climax.text: Some super-Earths sit in habitable zones. They're prime targets in the search for signs of life beyond Earth.

punchline.text: The galaxy's most common planet type. We don't have one. That's the puzzle.

quiz.question: What makes super-Earths unusual in the context of our solar system?
options: Our solar system has none, despite them being the most common planet type
They are the only planets known to have liquid water
They orbit farther from their stars than any planet in our system
